questions.questionText: Papillary thyroid carcinoma is the most common thyroid malignancy and is characterized by:
questions.optionB: Orphan Annie eye nuclei (optically clear nuclei), nuclear grooves, intranuclear inclusions, and psammoma bodies
questions.explanation: Papillary thyroid carcinoma: most common thyroid cancer (~80%). Nuclear features are diagnostic: Orphan Annie eye nuclei (optically clear, ground-glass nuclei), nuclear grooves, intranuclear cytoplasmic inclusions (pseudo-inclusions), psammoma bodies. Spreads via lymphatics (cervical lymph node metastasis common — still excellent prognosis). RET/PTC rearrangements, BRAF V600E mutations. Best prognosis among thyroid cancers. Risk factor: childhood radiation exposure. Reference: Robbins — Papillary Thyroid Cancer.

questions.questionText: Pheochromocytoma is a catecholamine-secreting tumor arising from:
questions.optionB: Adrenal medullary chromaffin cells (10% rule: bilateral, malignant, extra-adrenal, pediatric, familial)
questions.explanation: Pheochromocytoma: catecholamine-producing tumor of adrenal medulla (chromaffin cells). Classic '10% rule' (many now known to be higher): 10% bilateral, 10% malignant, 10% extra-adrenal (paraganglioma), 10% pediatric, 10% familial. Presentation: episodic hypertension (paroxysmal), headache, sweating, palpitations, pallor. Diagnosis: elevated urinary metanephrines/VMA, plasma free metanephrines. Associated syndromes: MEN 2A/2B, VHL, NF1, SDH mutations. Reference: Robbins — Pheochromocytoma.

questions.questionText: Hashimoto thyroiditis is the most common cause of hypothyroidism and is characterized by:
questions.optionB: Autoimmune lymphocytic infiltration with germinal centers, Hürthle cell (oxyphilic/oncocytic) change, and anti-TPO/anti-thyroglobulin antibodies
questions.explanation: Hashimoto thyroiditis: most common cause of hypothyroidism in iodine-sufficient areas. Autoimmune: anti-TPO (anti-microsomal, most sensitive) and anti-thyroglobulin antibodies. Histology: dense lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate with germinal centers, Hürthle cell metaplasia (enlarged eosinophilic granular cells with abundant mitochondria), follicular atrophy. May have transient hyperthyroidism initially (hashitoxicosis — release of stored hormone). Increased risk of thyroid lymphoma (MALT → DLBCL). Reference: Robbins — Hashimoto.

questions.questionText: Graves disease is the most common cause of hyperthyroidism and is caused by:
questions.optionB: Thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins (TSI) — IgG antibodies that activate TSH receptor
questions.explanation: Graves disease: autoimmune hyperthyroidism. TSI (thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins): IgG antibodies bind and activate TSH receptor → unregulated thyroid hormone production (Type II hypersensitivity). Triad: hyperthyroidism, diffuse goiter, exophthalmos (infiltrative ophthalmopathy — lymphocyte/GAG infiltration of orbital tissues). Also: pretibial myxedema, thyroid acropachy. Histology: hyperplastic follicles with scalloped colloid, papillary infoldings, lymphocytic infiltrate. TSH low, T3/T4 high. Treatment: ATDs, RAI, surgery. Reference: Robbins — Graves Disease.

questions.optionB: Medullary thyroid carcinoma + pheochromocytoma + parathyroid hyperplasia (RET proto-oncogene)
questions.explanation: MEN 2A (Sipple syndrome): AD, RET proto-oncogene mutations (chromosome 10). Triad: (1) Medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC — 100% penetrance, C-cell origin, calcitonin, amyloid stroma), (2) Pheochromocytoma (~50%), (3) Parathyroid hyperplasia/adenoma (~20%). MEN 2B: MTC + pheochromocytoma + mucosal neuromas + Marfanoid habitus (NO parathyroid disease). MEN 1 (Wermer): parathyroid + pancreatic (gastrinoma/insulinoma) + pituitary (MEN1/menin gene). Prophylactic thyroidectomy for RET carriers. Reference: Robbins — MEN 2A.

questions.questionText: Cushing syndrome is most commonly caused by:
questions.optionB: Exogenous glucocorticoid administration (iatrogenic); endogenous causes: ACTH-secreting pituitary adenoma (Cushing disease, most common endogenous cause)
questions.explanation: Cushing syndrome: hypercortisolism. Most common cause overall: iatrogenic (exogenous steroids). Most common endogenous cause: Cushing disease (ACTH-secreting pituitary microadenoma, ~70% of endogenous). Others: ectopic ACTH (small cell lung cancer — 15%), adrenal adenoma/carcinoma (15%). Features: central obesity, moon face, buffalo hump, striae, hypertension, hyperglycemia, osteoporosis, easy bruising. Diagnosis: 24-hr urinary cortisol, overnight dexamethasone suppression test. Reference: Robbins — Cushing Syndrome.

questions.questionText: Addison disease (primary adrenal insufficiency) is most commonly caused by:
questions.optionB: Autoimmune adrenalitis (destruction of adrenal cortex) in developed countries; TB in developing countries
questions.explanation: Addison disease: primary adrenal insufficiency (destruction of >90% of both adrenal cortices). Most common cause: autoimmune (anti-21-hydroxylase antibodies, developed countries), TB (developing countries). Both cortex layers affected → low cortisol + low aldosterone + elevated ACTH (→ hyperpigmentation from ACTH/MSH). Features: weakness, weight loss, hypotension, hyperkalemia, hyponatremia, hyperpigmentation, salt craving. Addisonian crisis: life-threatening. Treatment: corticosteroid + fludrocortisone replacement. Reference: Robbins — Addison Disease.

questions.questionText: Diabetes mellitus type 1 is caused by:
questions.optionB: Autoimmune destruction of pancreatic β cells → absolute insulin deficiency
questions.explanation: DM Type 1: autoimmune T cell-mediated destruction of pancreatic β cells (insulitis — lymphocytic infiltration of islets) → absolute insulin deficiency. Autoantibodies: anti-GAD65 (glutamic acid decarboxylase), ICA (islet cell antibodies), anti-insulin, anti-IA-2. HLA association: DR3, DR4 (HLA-DQ2, DQ8). Presents: young patients, polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, DKA. C-peptide: low/absent. Type 2: insulin resistance + relative deficiency, older, obesity-associated, non-ketotic. Reference: Robbins — DM Type 1.

questions.questionText: Prolactinoma is the most common type of pituitary adenoma and presents with:
questions.optionB: Hyperprolactinemia → amenorrhea, galactorrhea (women), decreased libido/infertility (men), treated with dopamine agonists
questions.explanation: Prolactinoma: most common functioning pituitary adenoma (~40%). Secretes prolactin → hyperprolactinemia. Women: amenorrhea, galactorrhea, infertility (present earlier, smaller tumors). Men: decreased libido, erectile dysfunction, infertility (present later, larger macroadenomas → visual field defects — bitemporal hemianopia from optic chiasm compression). Prolactin: ONLY pituitary hormone under tonic inhibition (by dopamine). Treatment: dopamine agonists (cabergoline, bromocriptine — first line, even for macroadenomas). Reference: Robbins — Prolactinoma.

questions.questionText: Conn syndrome (primary hyperaldosteronism) is most commonly caused by:
questions.explanation: Conn syndrome: primary hyperaldosteronism. Most common cause: aldosterone-producing adenoma (Conn's adenoma, ~65%). Bilateral idiopathic hyperplasia (~30%). Features: hypertension (resistant to standard treatment), hypokalemia (muscle weakness, cardiac arrhythmias), metabolic alkalosis. Low plasma renin activity (aldosterone acts independently of renin). ARR (aldosterone/renin ratio): screening test. CT adrenals + adrenal vein sampling for lateralization. Treatment: adrenalectomy (adenoma), spironolactone/eplerenone (hyperplasia). Reference: Robbins — Conn Syndrome.
